name: Codex Gigas
description: The Codex Gigas, also known as the Devil's Bible, is a large medieval manuscript originating from Bohemia (modern Czech Republic) in the early 13th century. It's renowned for its immense size, its elaborate illuminations, and the full-page depiction of the devil, leading to speculation about its origins and purpose. Proponents of alternative theories often suggest that the Codex Gigas was created through supernatural means, perhaps with the direct assistance of the devil, or that it contains hidden knowledge and secrets beyond its overt religious content. This stems from the legend that a monk condemned to be walled up alive made a deal with the devil to complete the book in a single night. Mainstream scholarship attributes the Codex Gigas to the Benedictine monastery of Podlažice. They believe it was likely the work of a single scribe over a period of many years, possibly decades. The inclusion of diverse texts, from the Old and New Testaments to medical treatises and historical chronicles, indicates a desire to create a comprehensive compendium of knowledge. The devil's image is interpreted by scholars not as evidence of demonic pacts, but as a representation of evil and a reminder of its presence within the world, consistent with medieval Christian iconography. The uniformity of the handwriting throughout the Codex suggests a single author, contradicting claims of supernatural assistance. The sheer scale and complexity of the project highlight the dedication and resources available within the monastery.
subtitle: The Devil's Bible: Mystery and Scholarship

claims.claim: The Codex Gigas was written in a single night with the Devil's help.
claims.evidence: The legend of the monk and the Devil, the uniformity of the script despite the immense size.
claims.criticism: The uniformity of the script suggests a single scribe working over many years. Radiocarbon dating of the parchment supports a long creation period, contradicting the single-night claim. The 'uniformity' can be attributed to meticulous planning and consistent handwriting style.

claims.claim: The Codex contains hidden codes or prophecies.
claims.evidence: The unusual inclusion of the Devil's image, the varied content of the book, and the possibility of secret symbolism.
claims.criticism: No demonstrable hidden codes or prophecies have been found despite extensive analysis. The content is consistent with a desire to compile a comprehensive encyclopedia of knowledge for the time. The Devil's image is a standard medieval motif.

claims.evidence: Its association with the Devil and its supposed ability to influence events.
claims.criticism: There is no scientific evidence to support claims of occult power. Attributing events to the Codex's influence relies on anecdotal evidence and superstition.

keyFigures.name: Hermann der Reichenau
keyFigures.role: Benedictine monk; his chronicle is included in the Codex

keyFigures.name: Michael Gullick
keyFigures.role: Palaeographer; expert on the Codex Gigas, offering scholarly analysis

mythologyConnections: Legends of pacts with the Devil

timeline.event: Codex Gigas is created at the Benedictine monastery of Podlažice, Bohemia.
Codex Gigas is moved to Prague by Rudolf II.
Codex Gigas is taken as war booty by the Swedish army during the Thirty Years' War.
Codex Gigas is temporarily returned to Prague for an exhibition.

sources.author: Michael Gullick
sources.work: The Codex Gigas: A Preliminary Description
sources.title: Michael Gullick

variants: The Eighth Wonder of the World
